books.title: About a Boy
books.author: Nick Hornby
books.synopsis: Will Freeman lives off his father's royalties and invents a child to join a single-parents group, where he meets Marcus — an awkward twelve-year-old whose mother is spiraling into depression.

themes: What does the novel say about masculinity and emotional vulnerability?
How does loneliness drive both Will and Marcus to change?
Is authenticity possible when relationships begin with deception?
characters: How does Marcus function as both comic relief and moral center?
What makes Will's transformation believable — or not?
How does Fiona's depression shape the story without defining her entirely?
symbols: What role does music (especially 'Killing Me Softly') play in the narrative?
How do the 'islands' metaphor — people who don't need anyone — evolve?
quotes: Discuss: 'No man is an island.' How does Hornby test this idea?
Which moment of dialogue best captures the odd chemistry between Will and Marcus?

books.title: About Grace
books.author: Anthony Doerr
books.synopsis: David Winkler, a hydrologist haunted by prophetic dreams, abandons his family to protect them from a disaster he foresees — then spends decades trying to find his way back.

themes: Can running away ever be an act of love?
How does Doerr weave science and mysticism together?
What does the novel suggest about second chances?
characters: Is David sympathetic despite his abandonment?
How does Grace's perspective change our reading of David's choices?
symbols: What do water and weather patterns symbolize throughout?
How does the snowflake motif connect to identity?
quotes: Find a passage about prediction — how does foreknowledge become a curse?

books.title: Absalom, Absalom!
books.author: William Faulkner
books.synopsis: Through nested narrators, the rise and ruin of Thomas Sutpen's dynasty in Mississippi unfolds — a Gothic epic of ambition, race, and the unreliability of memory.

themes: How does Faulkner explore the South's haunted past?
What is the cost of the 'design' Sutpen pursues?
Can history ever be told objectively in this novel?
characters: Compare Quentin and Shreve as listeners — why does the story need an audience?
What makes Sutpen both mythic and monstrous?
symbols: What does the mansion Sutpen builds represent?
How does the name 'Absalom' invoke biblical tragedy?
quotes: Choose a passage of breathless Faulkner prose — what does the style force readers to experience?

books.title: Alias Grace
books.author: Margaret Atwood
books.synopsis: Grace Marks, convicted of double murder in 1843 Canada, tells her story to a psychiatrist — but whose truth are we hearing?

themes: How does Atwood examine gender, class, and credibility?
Is Grace a victim, a performer, or both?
What does quilting symbolize as a narrative act?
characters: How trustworthy is Dr. Simon Jordan?
What do the servants and employers reveal about Victorian power?
symbols: Analyze the quilt patterns Grace sews — what stories do they tell?
What role do dreams and hypnosis play?
quotes: Discuss a moment where Grace withholds or reshapes the truth.

books.title: All About Love
books.author: bell hooks
books.synopsis: bell hooks argues that love is a transformative practice — not just a feeling — and examines how culture fails to teach us how to love.

themes: How does hooks redefine love as action rather than emotion?
What is the connection between love and justice?
How does childhood wounding shape adult relationships?
characters: hooks writes personally — how does her voice build trust with readers?
Which cultural figures does she invoke effectively?
symbols: What does hooks mean by 'love as a verb'?
How is the 'loveless culture' depicted?
quotes: Pick a definition of love from the text — do you agree?
Which passage challenged your assumptions most?

books.title: After Dark
books.author: Haruki Murakami
books.synopsis: Over a single Tokyo night, a sister reads in a Denny's while her sleeping sibling's spirit wanders — and strangers' lives intersect in the city's after-hours glow.

themes: What does Murakami suggest about the hidden life of cities at night?
How do dreams and waking reality blur?
What is the novel's stance on loneliness?
characters: Why is Mari our anchor in such a strange narrative?
How does the TV as a window function narratively?
symbols: What does the unplugged phone represent?
How does darkness operate as both setting and mood?
quotes: Discuss the novel's use of real time — how does the clock affect tension?

books.title: Beloved
books.author: Toni Morrison
books.synopsis: Sethe, a formerly enslaved woman, is haunted by the ghost of her dead child — until a mysterious young woman named Beloved arrives.

themes: How does Morrison make the unspeakable speakable?
What is the price of memory versus forgetting?
How does motherhood function under slavery's legacy?
characters: Who is Beloved — ghost, guilt, history, or all three?
How does Paul D's presence challenge Sethe's isolation?
symbols: What does 124 Bluestone Road represent?
Analyze the tree on Sethe's back.
quotes: Discuss: 'Freeing yourself was one thing; claiming ownership of that freed self was another.'

books.title: Brave New World
books.author: Aldous Huxley
books.synopsis: In a genetically engineered future of conditioned happiness, John the Savage challenges a world that traded suffering for stability.

themes: Is stability worth the cost of freedom?
How does consumerism replace religion?
Does the novel predict our present more than Orwell's 1984?
characters: Why is John the most tragic figure?
What does Mustapha Mond believe he's protecting?
symbols: What does soma represent in our culture today?
How does Shakespeare function as a symbol of the old world?
quotes: 'Wasn't it better to be unhappy but free?' — where do you stand?

books.title: Circe
books.author: Madeline Miller
books.synopsis: Banished to a deserted island, the witch Circe discovers her power — and reclaims her story from the men who mythologized her.

themes: How is exile transformed into freedom?
What does the novel say about immortality versus mortality?
How does Miller feminist-retell myth?
characters: How does Circe change from timid nymph to sovereign?
Which mythic guest (Odysseus, Medea, Penelope) reveals most about her?
symbols: What does witchcraft represent as craft and agency?
How does Aiaia function as a character?
quotes: Discuss Circe's definition of power versus Zeus's.

books.title: Dune
books.author: Frank Herbert
books.synopsis: Paul Atreides arrives on desert planet Arrakis, where spice, prophecy, and ecological politics collide in an epic of empire.

themes: Is Paul a hero or a warning about messianic leadership?
How does ecology function as politics?
What does the novel say about colonial extraction?
characters: How do the Bene Gesserit shape history from the shadows?
What makes Lady Jessica compelling?
symbols: What does the sandworm represent?
Analyze water as the ultimate wealth.
quotes: 'Fear is the mind-killer' — how does the litany against fear work in the story?

books.title: Educated
books.author: Tara Westover
books.synopsis: Raised in a survivalist Idaho family that distrusted formal schooling, Westover educates herself to Cambridge — and must reckon with the family she leaves behind.

themes: What is the relationship between education and self-invention?
How does Westover write about family loyalty without villainizing?
Can you ever fully leave your upbringing?
characters: How does Shawn function as both loving brother and abuser?
What role does Tyler play as a bridge?
symbols: What does the junkyard symbolize?
How does the mountain function as home and prison?
quotes: Discuss a moment where Westover's memory is uncertain — why include it?

books.title: Frankenstein
books.author: Mary Shelley
books.synopsis: Victor Frankenstein creates life from dead matter, then abandons his Creature — setting off a tragedy of rejection, revenge, and responsibility.

themes: Who is the real monster?
What are the limits of scientific ambition?
How does the novel explore loneliness?
characters: Does Victor deserve sympathy?
How does the Creature's eloquence change our moral judgment?
symbols: What does fire symbolize for the Creature?
How does the Arctic frame function?
quotes: 'Beware; for I am fearless, and therefore powerful.' — discuss power born from suffering.

books.title: The Great Gatsby
books.author: F. Scott Fitzgerald
books.synopsis: Nick Carraway watches his mysterious neighbor Jay Gatsby pursue wealth and Daisy Buchanan across one glittering, doomed Long Island summer.

themes: Is the American Dream aspirational or destructive here?
How does class shape every relationship?
What is the role of nostalgia?
characters: Why is Nick the narrator — is he reliable?
What makes Gatsby both magnificent and hollow?
symbols: Analyze the green light.
What do the eyes of Dr. T.J. Eckleburg represent?
quotes: 'So we beat on, boats against the current...' — what current?

books.title: The Handmaid's Tale
books.author: Margaret Atwood
books.synopsis: In the Republic of Gilead, Offred serves as a reproductive slave — her inner life the last private rebellion against a theocratic state.

themes: How does Gilead weaponize language?
What forms of resistance exist when open rebellion is death?
How relevant is this novel today?
characters: What do we learn from Offred's memories of Luke and Moira?
How does Serena Joy embody complicity?
symbols: What do the red habits signify?
Analyze the Wall.
quotes: 'Nolite te bastardes carborundorum' — what does this Latin joke mean for hope?

books.title: Invisible Man
books.author: Ralph Ellison
books.synopsis: A young Black man moves from the Jim Crow South to Harlem, discovering that society renders him invisible while demanding he perform visibility.

themes: What kinds of invisibility does Ellison explore?
How does the Brotherhood replicate oppression?
Is the ending hopeful or resigned?
characters: How does the protagonist remain unnamed — and why?
What does Brother Jack's glass eye symbolize about ideology?
symbols: Analyze the briefcase and its contents.
What does the basement 'hole' represent?
quotes: 'I am invisible, understand, simply because people refuse to see me.' — personal resonance?

books.title: Jane Eyre
books.author: Charlotte Brontë
books.synopsis: Orphan Jane Eyre becomes governess at Thornfield Hall, where she falls for the brooding Rochester — and uncovers a devastating secret.

themes: How does Jane insist on moral equality in love?
What is the Gothic doing in a bildungsroman?
Does Jane sacrifice or assert autonomy at the end?
characters: Is Rochester redeemable?
How does Bertha Mason function in the story — and what are we to make of her portrayal?
symbols: Fire and ice imagery — track it through the novel.
What does the red room represent?
quotes: 'I am no bird; and no net ensnares me.' — discuss independence.

books.title: The Kite Runner
books.author: Khaled Hosseini
books.synopsis: Amir grows up in Kabul with his servant's son Hassan, betrays him, and years later seeks redemption in Taliban-era Afghanistan.

themes: Can guilt be redeemed through action?
How does the novel portray class and ethnic hierarchy?
What is the cost of silence?
characters: Is Amir's cowardice relatable or unforgivable?
How does Hassan's loyalty define the moral center?
symbols: What do kites represent at different stages?
How does the cleft lip motif work?
quotes: 'For you, a thousand times over' — when does this line land differently?

books.title: The Lottery
books.author: Shirley Jackson
books.synopsis: A small American village gathers for an annual lottery with a horrifying tradition that no one questions — until someone does.

themes: How does tradition justify cruelty?
What does the story say about scapegoating?
Why is the horror in the mundane details?
characters: How does Tessie Hutchinson's protest change the ending?
What do the children foreshadow?
symbols: Analyze the black box.
What does the stones imagery accomplish?
quotes: 'Lottery in June, corn be heavy soon' — discuss ritual and agriculture.

books.title: To Kill a Mockingbird
books.author: Harper Lee
books.synopsis: Scout Finch grows up in Depression-era Alabama as her father Atticus defends a Black man falsely accused of rape.

themes: How does innocence confront injustice?
What is the novel's vision of empathy?
How do we read this text today?
characters: Is Atticus a perfect moral guide or a complicated one?
How does Boo Radley function as symbol and person?
symbols: What does the mockingbird represent?
Analyze the Radley house.
quotes: 'You never really understand a person until you climb into his skin...' — limits of this metaphor?

books.author: George Orwell
books.synopsis: Winston Smith rebels against Big Brother's totalitarian surveillance state — and learns how thoroughly power can reshape reality itself.

themes: How does language control thought?
Is Winston's defeat inevitable?
What parallels do you see in contemporary surveillance culture?
characters: What does Julia want that Winston doesn't?
How does O'Brien embody the system's intelligence?
symbols: What does the glass paperweight represent?
Analyze Room 101.
quotes: 'War is peace, freedom is slavery, ignorance is strength' — how does doublethink work?

books.title: The Odyssey
books.author: Homer
books.synopsis: Odysseus struggles homeward for ten years after the Trojan War, battling gods, monsters, and his own hunger for home.

themes: What defines home — place, people, or identity?
How is hospitality (xenia) a moral law?
Is Odysseus a hero or a trickster?
characters: How does Penelope match Odysseus in cunning?
What is Telemachus's arc?
symbols: What does the sea represent?
Analyze the bed rooted in the olive tree.
quotes: Discuss a moment where Odysseus weeps — what makes him cry?

books.title: Pride and Prejudice
books.author: Jane Austen
books.synopsis: Elizabeth Bennet navigates manners, marriage, and misunderstanding with the proud Mr. Darcy in Regency England.

themes: How does Austen critique class and marriage markets?
What must both Elizabeth and Darcy unlearn?
Is this a romance or a social novel?
characters: Why is Elizabeth one of literature's great protagonists?
How does Wickham function as a foil?
symbols: What do Pemberley and Longbourn represent?
Track eyes and seeing throughout.
quotes: 'Till this moment I never knew myself.' — discuss self-knowledge.

books.title: Rebecca
books.author: Daphne du Maurier
books.synopsis: The second Mrs. de Winter arrives at Manderley haunted by the glamorous, dead Rebecca — and the secrets Maximilian de Winter hides.

themes: How does jealousy construct identity?
What does the novel say about gendered power in marriage?
Is Rebecca the villain or the victim?
characters: Why do we never learn the narrator's name?
How does Mrs. Danvers embody Rebecca's ghost?
symbols: Analyze Manderley as character.
What does the costume ball represent?
quotes: 'Last night I dreamt I went to Manderley again.' — why is this opening unforgettable?

books.title: Sula
books.author: Toni Morrison
books.synopsis: In the Bottom, a Black Ohio neighborhood, Nel and Sula's lifelong friendship ruptures when Sula returns as a dangerous nonconformist.

themes: What does friendship cost between women?
How does a community define evil?
What is the relationship between freedom and loneliness?
characters: Is Sula a villain or an avatar of freedom?
How does Nel's conformity shape her choices?
symbols: What does the plague of robins signify?
Analyze the tunnel disaster.
quotes: Discuss Morrison's line about how people die — what makes it true or harsh?

books.title: Things Fall Apart
books.author: Chinua Achebe
books.synopsis: Okonkwo, a proud Igbo warrior, watches his world collapse under colonial Christianity and British administration.

themes: How does Achebe counter colonial narratives?
What causes things to 'fall apart' — internal or external forces?
Is Okonkwo a tragic hero?
characters: How does Nwoye's conversion divide father and son?
What does Mr. Brown represent versus Rev. Smith?
symbols: What do yams represent?
Analyze the locusts.
quotes: Discuss the District Commissioner's planned title — why is it devastating?

books.title: Ulysses
books.author: James Joyce
books.synopsis: Leopold Bloom wanders Dublin on June 16, 1904, in parallel with Homer's Odyssey — one ordinary day rendered infinite.

themes: How does Joyce elevate the mundane?
What is the novel's relationship to Homer?
Is Bloom heroic?
characters: How do Stephen and Bloom mirror each other?
What makes Molly's soliloquy a feminist landmark?
symbols: Track water and burial imagery.
What does the lack of a home key mean for Bloom?
quotes: Pick one sentence from Molly's monologue — what does stream-of-consciousness unlock?

books.title: The Virgin Suicides
books.author: Jeffrey Eugenides
books.synopsis: The Lisbon sisters fascinate their suburban neighborhood — until, one by one, they die by suicide, remembered only by the boys who obsessed over them.

themes: Who owns the girls' story — the narrators or the girls?
How does suburbia become Gothic?
What does the novel say about male gaze and myth-making?
characters: Can we know any Lisbon sister as an individual?
How do the parents' repression drive tragedy?
symbols: Analyze the fence and the house.
What do the prom dresses represent?
quotes: Discuss the plural 'we' narrator — what does it reveal about memory?

books.title: Wuthering Heights
books.author: Emily Brontë
books.synopsis: Heathcliff and Catherine's destructive passion echoes across two generations on the Yorkshire moors.

themes: Is this a love story or a revenge tragedy?
How does the moors shape temperament?
Can passion transcend death in this novel?
characters: Is Heathcliff a Byronic hero or a monster?
How does Nelly Dean shape our judgment?
symbols: What do the two houses — Wuthering Heights and Thrushcross Grange — represent?
Analyze the window Catherine appears at.
quotes: 'I am Heathcliff' — what does Catherine mean?

books.title: The Yellow Wallpaper
books.author: Charlotte Perkins Gilman
books.synopsis: A woman confined to a nursery for a 'rest cure' becomes obsessed with the room's wallpaper — and what moves behind it.

themes: How does medical patriarchy silence women?
What is the relationship between creativity and madness?
Is the ending liberation or breakdown?
characters: How does John embody well-meaning cruelty?
Who is the woman in the wallpaper?
symbols: Track the wallpaper's evolution.
What does the barred window represent?
quotes: 'I've got out at last...' — discuss the final line.

books.title: The Zoo Story
books.author: Edward Albee
books.synopsis: On a Central Park bench, Peter meets Jerry, whose bizarre confession escalates into violent connection.

themes: What does loneliness drive people to do?
How do class differences shape the encounter?
Is connection possible without pain?
characters: Why does Jerry need Peter?
Is Peter complicit in the ending?
symbols: What does the dog story allegorize?
How is the zoo a metaphor for modern life?
quotes: Discuss Jerry's line about going to the zoo — what is he really seeking?

books.title: The Iliad
books.author: Homer
books.synopsis: Achilles' wrath shapes the final year of the Trojan War — an epic of honor, mortality, and the cost of heroic pride.

themes: What is the poem's argument about glory versus life?
How does fate (moira) interact with human choice?
Does the Iliad celebrate or mourn war?
characters: How does Achilles change from withdrawal to compassion?
What makes Hector the poem's moral counterweight?
How do the gods function as audience and instigators?
symbols: Track armor, shields, and funeral rites.
What does the shield of Achilles represent as a world in miniature?
quotes: Discuss Achilles' choice of two fates — short glory or long obscurity.

books.title: The Aeneid
books.author: Virgil
books.synopsis: Aeneas flees fallen Troy to found Rome, torn between duty to destiny and love for Dido of Carthage.

themes: How does Virgil rewrite Homeric epic for Roman imperial identity?
Is pietas (duty) a virtue or a cruelty?
How does the poem mourn what empire destroys?
characters: Is Aeneas a hero or an instrument of fate?
How does Dido's tragedy reframe the founding myth?
What is Turnus's claim to our sympathy?
symbols: Analyze the golden bough.
How do funeral games and prophecies structure destiny?
quotes: 'I sing of arms and the man' — how does Virgil's opening set the poem's tensions?

books.title: Agamemnon
books.author: Aeschylus
books.synopsis: King Agamemnon returns from Troy to Argos, where Clytemnestra's vengeance for their daughter Iphigenia awaits.

themes: How does the Oresteia explore justice versus blood vengeance?
What role does hubris play in royal return?
How does Aeschylus stage gender and power?
characters: Is Clytemnestra a monster, a judge, or both?
How does the Chorus mediate our moral response?
What does Cassandra see that others cannot?
symbols: Track the net, the axe, and the crimson path.
What does the watchman's beacon opening signify?
quotes: Discuss the carpet scene — invitation or trap?

books.title: Ajax
books.author: Sophocles
books.synopsis: Humiliated when Achilles' armor is awarded to Odysseus, Ajax descends into madness and confronts the limits of heroic honor.

themes: What happens when public honor collapses?
How does Sophocles treat suicide and legacy?
Is Ajax's rigidity tragic or self-destructive?
characters: How does Tecmessa humanize Ajax?
What does Odysseus's mercy at the end accomplish?
symbols: Analyze the sword Ajax receives from Hector.
What does madness look like on stage?
quotes: Discuss Ajax's speech on inner versus outer excellence.

books.title: Oedipus Rex
books.author: Sophocles
books.synopsis: Oedipus investigates a plague in Thebes and uncovers the horrifying truth of his own identity.

themes: Is Oedipus punished for fate or for the passion of his inquiry?
How does knowledge function as tragedy?
What is the play's theory of political leadership?
characters: How does Tiresias know differently than Oedipus sees?
What does Jocasta try to stop — and why?
symbols: Track blindness and sight throughout.
What do crossroads represent?
quotes: 'Count no man happy till he dies' — when does this land?

books.title: Lysistrata
books.author: Aristophanes
books.synopsis: Athenian and Spartan women withhold intimacy to force an end to the Peloponnesian War — bawdy, political, and surprisingly strategic.

themes: Can comedy accomplish what tragedy cannot?
How does Aristophanes satirize war and masculinity?
Is Lysistrata a feminist hero or a comic device?
characters: How does Lysistrata wield language as power?
What do the Magistrate and old men represent?
symbols: Analyze the Acropolis occupation.
What does the weaving metaphor accomplish?
quotes: Pick a passage where innuendo carries political argument.

books.title: The Symposium
books.author: Plato
books.synopsis: At a drinking party, Athenian intellectuals deliver competing speeches on the nature of love — culminating in Socrates' ladder of beauty.

themes: What are the competing Greek theories of eros?
How does Plato connect love to philosophy?
Why frame philosophy as after-dinner entertainment?
characters: How does Alcibiades disrupt the order of speeches?
What makes Diotima's voice radical in the dialogue?
symbols: What does the symposium setting imply about truth and pleasure?
Track ascending imagery in Socrates' account.
quotes: Discuss Aristophanes' myth of the split halves — still useful today?

books.title: The Republic
books.author: Plato
books.synopsis: Socrates investigates justice in the soul and the city, proposing the philosopher-king and the famous allegory of the cave.

themes: Is Plato's ideal city liberating or authoritarian?
How does the cave allegory define education?
What is the relationship between art and truth in Book X?
characters: Why does Thrasymachus challenge Socrates so aggressively?
How does Glaucon's ring of Gyges test morality?
symbols: Analyze the divided line and the cave.
What does the myth of Er accomplish at the close?
quotes: 'The untroubled city' — can we read Book V without modern discomfort?

books.title: Meditations
books.author: Marcus Aurelius
books.synopsis: The Roman emperor's private notebooks on Stoic discipline, mortality, and governing oneself before governing others.

themes: How does Stoicism address power and impermanence?
Is Marcus writing for himself or posterity?
What is the Stoic relationship to emotion?
characters: How does Marcus portray other people — enemies, colleagues, the gods?
What kind of self does the text construct?
symbols: Track cosmic scale imagery.
What does the 'inner citadel' mean?
quotes: Choose one maxim — does it hold under pressure?

books.title: After Virtue
books.author: Alasdair MacIntyre
books.synopsis: MacIntyre argues modern moral discourse is in fragments and revives Aristotelian virtue ethics as a coherent alternative.

themes: Is moral language in crisis?
How does MacIntyre use historical narrative as argument?
What would a practice-based ethics look like in art history?
characters: How does MacIntyre stage rival traditions — Aristotle, the Enlightenment, Nietzsche?
symbols: What does the 'catastrophe' of the Enlightenment represent?
Analyze the Benedictine monastery epilogue.
quotes: Discuss the claim that we live among moral fragments.

books.title: The Histories
books.author: Herodotus
books.synopsis: Herodotus investigates the clash of Greeks and Persians — and the customs of peoples across the known world — inventing history as inquiry.

themes: What is Herodotus's method — storytelling or evidence?
How does he explain freedom versus tyranny?
Why does ethnography sit beside battle narrative?
characters: How does Xerxes embody imperial overreach?
What makes Artemisia memorable?
How does Themistocles operate as strategist?
symbols: Track omens, dreams, and oracles.
What do bridges and rivers signify in Persian campaigns?
quotes: Discuss a digression — why does Herodotus wander from the main war?

books.title: History of the Peloponnesian War
books.author: Thucydides
books.synopsis: Thucydides chronicles the devastating war between Athens and Sparta with speeches, sieges, and the plague — aiming for lasting political truth.

themes: How does Thucydides differ from Herodotus?
What does the Melian Dialogue say about power?
Is Pericles' funeral oration sincere propaganda?
characters: How does Alcibiades embody Athenian instability?
What does Nicias's caution cost?
symbols: Analyze the plague as civic metaphor.
What does the Sicilian expedition represent?
quotes: 'The strong do what they can' — is Thucydides cynical or realistic?

books.title: Agricola
books.author: Tacitus
books.synopsis: Tacitus memorializes his father-in-law Agricola, Roman governor of Britain — a study in virtuous service under a tyrannical emperor.

themes: How does biography become political critique?
What does Tacitus say about empire and colonization?
Can virtue survive under Domitian?
characters: How is Agricola idealized?
What role does Calgacus's speech play?
symbols: Track Britain as edge-of-world imagery.
What does silence under tyranny mean?
quotes: Discuss Calgacus on Roman peace as desolation.

books.title: SPQR
books.author: Mary Beard
books.synopsis: Mary Beard retells a thousand years of Roman history — from mythic origins to Caracalla — with attention to ordinary lives and contested sources.

themes: How does Beard demythologize Rome without diminishing it?
What does SPQR mean as a political slogan?
How do we read Roman history in the 21st century?
characters: How does Beard treat figures like Cicero or the Gracchi?
What do slaves and women reveal about Roman society?
symbols: Analyze the Colosseum as cultural symbol.
What do coins and inscriptions add to narrative?
quotes: Discuss Beard's approach to fragmentary evidence.

books.title: Metamorphoses
books.author: Ovid
books.synopsis: Ovid's kaleidoscopic poem links mythic tales of transformation from creation to Julius Caesar — witty, violent, and endlessly influential.

themes: Why is transformation Ovid's central metaphor?
How does the poem handle power and vulnerability?
How has Ovid shaped Western art?
characters: Compare Daphne, Arachne, and Philomela as artists/victims.
How does Orpheus function across books?
symbols: Track bodies becoming trees, stones, constellations.
What does the poem's frame from chaos to Rome accomplish?
quotes: Pick one myth — how would you illustrate it?

books.title: The Marriage of Cadmus and Harmony
books.author: Roberto Calasso
books.synopsis: Calasso retells Greek myth as a luminous essay — gods, mortals, and stories that explain how the West imagines itself.

themes: Is myth a form of knowledge?
How does Calasso blend scholarship and narrative?
What is the marriage of Cadmus and Harmony?
characters: How does Calasso treat Zeus, Athena, and Dionysus differently?
Why center Thetis and Achilles?
symbols: Track weddings, sacrifices, and origins.
How do images from vase painting enter the prose?
quotes: Discuss Calasso's claim that myth is what's left when everything else is explained.

books.title: I, Claudius
books.author: Robert Graves
books.synopsis: The stammering, overlooked Claudius narrates the Julio-Claudian dynasty — Livia's intrigues, Tiberius's cruelty, and the making of an unlikely emperor.

themes: How does Graves use fiction to fill historiographic gaps?
What is the politics of the imperial family?
Can the fool survive the palace?
characters: Is Livia the true architect of power?
How does Claudius's disability shape perception?
symbols: Track writing and historiography as survival.
What do omens and prophecies do in the novel?
quotes: Discuss Claudius's relationship to Tacitus and Suetonius.

books.title: Ways of Seeing
books.author: John Berger
books.synopsis: Berger's landmark book and BBC series dismantle how we look at oil paintings, advertising, and the female nude — linking aesthetics to power.

themes: How does reproduction change what images mean?
What is the male gaze in Berger's terms?
How does capitalism alter visual culture?
characters: Berger writes as teacher — how does his voice position the viewer?
symbols: Compare original paintings with advertisements.
What do mirrors and poses signify?
quotes: 'Seeing comes before words' — do you agree?

books.title: The Story of Art
books.author: E.H. Gombrich
books.synopsis: Gombrich's accessible survey traces world art from prehistoric caves to modern experiment — emphasizing innovation within tradition.

themes: How does Gombrich define artistic 'progress'?
What is gained and lost in a single narrative of art?
How does he treat non-Western art?
characters: Which artist biographies does Gombrich foreground — and why?
symbols: Pick one plate — how does Gombrich read form and function?
What does 'the Beholder's share' imply?
quotes: Discuss 'There really is no such thing as Art' — what is he arguing?

books.title: The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction
books.author: Walter Benjamin
books.synopsis: Benjamin argues that photography and film destroy the 'aura' of unique artworks — transforming politics, tradition, and how masses perceive art.

themes: What is aura — and do digital images have it?
How does reproduction democratize or cheapen art?
What is Benjamin's theory of fascism and aesthetics?
characters: How does Benjamin stage Marx, Baudelaire, and the collector?
symbols: Analyze the cult value versus exhibition value of images.
What does the cathedral in the age of tourism mean?
quotes: Discuss 'the urge to bring things closer' in the age of Instagram.

books.title: On Photography
books.author: Susan Sontag
books.synopsis: Sontag's essays examine how photographs alter our ethics, memory, and relationship to suffering — from surrealism to war reporting.

themes: Do photographs explain or colonize experience?
What is the ethics of looking at pain?
How has the image world changed since 1977?
characters: How does Sontag write as cultural critic versus artist's ally?
symbols: Track cameras, albums, and postcards.
What does the 'image-world' mean today?
quotes: 'To photograph is to appropriate' — still true in the selfie era?

books.title: Lives of the Artists
books.author: Giorgio Vasari
books.synopsis: Vasari's pioneering artist biographies from Cimabue to Michelangelo invent art history as genre — blending anecdote, gossip, and Renaissance triumph.

themes: How does Vasari create the myth of the Renaissance?
What is the relationship between biography and evaluation?
Which anecdotes are fabrication — and why?
characters: Compare Giotto, Leonardo, and Michelangelo as Vasari shapes them.
How does Vasari himself enter the story?
symbols: Track progress from 'rude' Gothic to refined classicism.
What do workshops and patronage reveal?
quotes: Discuss Vasari on Giotto drawing a perfect O — myth or method?

books.title: The Agony and the Ecstasy
books.author: Irving Stone
books.synopsis: Irving Stone dramatizes Michelangelo's life — from Carrara marble to the Sistine ceiling — as art, faith, and patronage collide.

themes: How does fiction fill gaps in artistic biography?
What is the cost of genius in Stone's portrait?
How do popes and politics shape masterpieces?
characters: How is Michelangelo both rebel and servant?
What role does Vittoria Colonna play?
symbols: Track marble, scaffolding, and unfinished work.
What does the Sistine ceiling mean as labor?
quotes: Discuss a scene of physical strain — how does Stone embody making?

books.title: Girl with a Pearl Earring
books.author: Tracy Chevalier
books.synopsis: A fictional servant girl becomes the subject of Vermeer's painting in 17th-century Delft — exploring gaze, class, and the silence of images.

themes: Who owns the story behind a portrait?
How does Chevalier imagine the studio as social space?
What is the erotics of looking in Vermeer?
characters: How does Griet negotiate household power?
What does Vermeer see that others miss?
symbols: Analyze the pearl, the turban, and the crack in the palette.
What does grinding pigment represent?
quotes: Discuss the moment Griet pierces her ear — consent and spectacle.

books.title: Pompeii
books.author: Robert Harris
books.synopsis: An engineer races against Vesuvius's eruption in 79 CE — Harris reconstructs Roman urban life from aqueducts to the forum.

themes: How does disaster fiction illuminate daily antiquity?
What does infrastructure reveal about empire?
How do we romanticize Pompeii?
characters: How does Attilius embody Roman practical virtue?
What do Pliny the Elder and corrupt officials represent?
symbols: Track water, ash, and the volcano as character.
What does the amphitheater scene signify?
quotes: Discuss Harris's use of archaeological detail as plot engine.

books.title: Rubicon
books.author: Tom Holland
books.synopsis: Holland narrates the fall of the Roman Republic — from Sulla to Augustus — with the pace of political thriller and classical sweep.

themes: Why does the Republic collapse?
How does Holland make ancient politics contemporary?
What is the moral of Caesar's crossing?
characters: Compare Caesar, Pompey, and Cicero's ambitions.
How does Cleopatra function in Roman narrative?
symbols: What does the Rubicon represent as point of no return?
Track blood, proscription, and triumph.
quotes: Discuss Holland's opening — why start with Sulla's severed head?

books.title: The Etruscans Uncovered
books.author: UC Berkeley Hearst Collection
books.synopsis: Companion to the Brown Gallery exhibit on the Phoebe A. Hearst Collection — Berkeley's Etruscan artifacts and what they reveal about pre-Roman Italy.

themes: Why are the Etruscans overshadowed by Greece and Rome?
How do museum collections shape scholarship?
What does Hearst's collecting era tell us?
characters: Who were the Etruscan elites buried in these objects?
How do curators narrate incomplete archives?
symbols: Analyze funerary urns, bronzes, and painted tombs.
What does excavation photography add?
quotes: Discuss one object label — what story does the museum choose to tell?

books.title: Against Interpretation
books.author: Susan Sontag
books.synopsis: Sontag's influential essays champion an erotics of art over hermeneutics — from camp and style to Godard and science fiction.

themes: What does Sontag mean by 'against interpretation'?
How does camp subvert seriousness?
Is transparency of content a false goal for art?
characters: How does Sontag position herself among critics and artists?
symbols: Track surfaces, style, and silence.
What does 'notes on camp' redefine?
quotes: 'In place of a hermeneutics we need an erotics of art' — provocative or useful?
